name: Dietary Sensitivity & Nutrition
description: Lactose, caffeine, alcohol, salt, omega-3 conversion, and bitter-taste variants.

snps.description: T allele keeps lactase expressed into adulthood (Northern European origin).
A allele is the fast-metabolizer variant; C allele is slow metabolizer (higher CV risk from caffeine).

AC.note: Slow metabolizer — caffeine persists longer; avoid late-day intake.

CC.note: Slow metabolizer — high-dose caffeine may raise CV risk.

snps.description: A allele (common in East Asian ancestry) causes acetaldehyde buildup and flushing response.

AG.note: Intermediate metabolism — flushing, elevated esophageal cancer risk with regular drinking.

AA.note: Near-absent acetaldehyde clearance — strong flush, high cancer risk with regular drinking.

snps.description: The A allele encodes a faster ADH1B enzyme that can make alcohol feel more aversive and lower heavy-drinking tendency, especially when paired with ALDH2 variants.

AG.note: Faster ADH1B pattern — alcohol may feel less rewarding or more unpleasant, and acetaldehyde buildup can feel stronger if ALDH2 is also reduced.

AA.note: Strongest common fast-ADH1B pattern — alcohol is often metabolized to acetaldehyde quickly, which can amplify flushing or aversive effects when ALDH2 is also reduced.

snps.description: Controls ALA to EPA/DHA conversion efficiency. T allele = reduced conversion.

GT.note: Moderate conversion — marine omega-3 more important.

snps.description: Detects bitter thiourea compounds in cruciferous vegetables.

CC.note: Taster — may dislike Brussels sprouts, broccoli.

GG.note: Non-taster — less sensitivity to bitter compounds.
